title: The F#ck It Switch: How I Broke Every Rule and Won a National Title
summary: At 14, Seth Pepper walked up to a coach and said he wanted to go to the Olympics — he didn't yet know how to swim — and in this solo episode he traces the path from that moment to a national championship, unpacking the Performance Equation, the Eff It Switch, and the Pablo Morales story that "never heals" along the way.
showNotes:
For the first solo episode of *Unlimit Your Potential*, Seth Pepper turns the chair around on himself. No guest, no script — just the story behind the show, the framework behind the coaching, and the moment behind the title.

At fourteen, with no swimming background, Seth walked up to a YMCA head coach in a small-town basement pool and told him he wanted to go to the Olympics. The coach laughed. Six years later Seth was racing the Olympic gold medalist; eight years later he was a National Champion. The episode is the throughline between those two moments — what he was reading on the wall, what his coach Rick DeMont was whispering after his second-place finishes, and what flipped in the seconds before his final NCAA final.

## The performance equation

> Your ultimate performance equals your ability minus resistance.

Most resistance is self-inflicted. The job, then, is helping people get out of their own way — which is also Seth's one-line answer when people ask what he does for a living.

## Key takeaways

- **Pressure is just anticipation in your body** — and the mind's only job in that moment is to guess what happens next. The guesses are usually wrong. Awareness of the mechanic is the way out.
- **The subconscious is the supercomputer.** Riding a bike, driving a car, missing turns at full speed — you let the system run. The trick is programming it visually and consistently long before the race.
- **The Eff It Switch is acceptance, not aggression.** Breaking the external rules is easy. Breaking the internal ones is where the transcendence happens.
- **Failure gives you the most information.** Reframe it as the fastest path to growth and you've removed the biggest source of resistance in one move.
- **Power hides in the obvious.** The greats aren't doing anything secret — they're staying with the simple thing longer than anyone else.

## Notable quotes

> "You are either writing the story, or the story is writing you."

> "Feelings are not facts. They feel real because they're your feelings."

> "I am not going to teach you anything new. You already know this stuff. I am just bringing it to the top, and we're going to keep it at the top."

## Resources & references

- **The Australian basketball mental-training study** — three groups, three months, free throws. The mental-only group improved 23%, almost matching the physical-only group's 24%.
- **Pablo Morales** — the Olympic butterfly champion on Seth's bedroom wall, who later handed Seth his National Championship trophy, then came out of retirement and took the Olympic spot Seth was chasing. "The story that never heals."
- **Rick DeMont** — Seth's sprinting coach at the University of Arizona. Former world record holder. The voice behind *"Why are you apologizing for your greatness?"* and *"Why don't you just go take it?"*
- **Eric Heiden** — the speed skater who won five gold medals at Lake Placid, Seth's first Olympic obsession.
- **Joseph Campbell** — *The Hero with a Thousand Faces*, the mythology behind "trust the force."
- **Miracle on Ice** — *"Do you believe in miracles?"* — the moment that planted the seed when Seth was a kid.

## About this episode

This is the origin story. Subsequent episodes go inside the rooms with Olympians, World Champions, NFL quarterbacks, MLB performance directors, PGA Tour coaches and CBS analysts. The frameworks Seth uses with them — the Performance Equation, the Effort Switch, the simple loop of evidence and accountability — start here.

title: From Undrafted to the NFL: Chris Streveler on Mindset, Visualization & Resilience
summary: NFL quarterback Chris Streveler joins Seth to talk about the road less traveled: from Division I college football through two CFL Grey Cup Championships to the NFL. A masterclass in resilience, mental performance under uncertainty, and what it takes to keep showing up when the path isn't linear.
showNotes:
Chris Streveler shouldn't have made the NFL. He went undrafted out of South Dakota — no minicamp invite, no tryout. He went to Winnipeg, won a Grey Cup, scratched his way onto a Cardinals roster, got cut by phone call two days before Thanksgiving while leaked on Twitter, signed with Baltimore four days later, got cut, signed with Miami, got cut again, signed with the Jets — and then threw two touchdowns and a 17-second game-winner in his first preseason action of camp three weeks deep without a single rep.

This conversation is what it actually takes to stay in the room.

## Key takeaways

- **"Stay off the roller coaster."** A CFL teammate gave Chris his most-quoted line as a rookie. The quarterback's job, and the leader's job, is to hold the middle — bring people up when they're down, bring them down to reality when they're high.
- **Adversity reveals character; it doesn't build it.** Anyone can be the high-energy guy when they're undefeated and throwing touchdowns. The question is who you are after four picks and a four-game losing streak.
- **Be where your feet are.** Maybe you only have 75% to give that day — give 100% of that 75%. Same answer for a podcast as for a one-year-old daughter at home.
- **Control the controllables: attitude, effort, work ethic.** Chris's three-item daily list. If those land, the rest sorts itself out.
- **Drop the expectations to access the flow.** The Jets preseason story is the masterclass — he played his best football the week he was certain he'd be cut.

## Notable quotes

> "If you're trying to see everything, you're seeing nothing. If you're trying to see a little bit, you're going to see everything." — Aaron Rodgers, on what Chris took from his year in New York

> "There is literally millions of people that want your job. So what are you doing today to keep that and stay in front of them?"

> "I was a guy who probably never should have made it to the NFL, and I did. So if I can make it to the NFL, there's no reason that, if I apply the same work ethic to the next phase, I can't have success there too."

## Resources & references

- **Aaron Rodgers** — Chris's year in the Jets QB room, and the eye-discipline principle that reshaped how he reads coverages
- **Lamar Jackson** — the contrast study in greatness: childlike enthusiasm, energy as superpower
- **Tom Brady & *The Brady Six*** — the documentary about the six quarterbacks drafted ahead of him
- **Mamba Mentality** — the 4 a.m. workout math: ten extra minutes a day, three hundred and sixty-five more reps a year
- **Going Steady** — the values-based dating app Chris co-founded with his fiancée, launched in Arizona
- The **Butterfly Effect** event in Arizona — where Chris and Seth first connected

## About the guest

Chris Streveler is a retired NFL quarterback whose path ran through Minnesota → South Dakota (FCS national player of the year) → the CFL (two Grey Cup Championships with the Winnipeg Blue Bombers) → the NFL, with stops at the Cardinals, Ravens, Dolphins, Jets, and Bills. He recently announced his retirement after back-to-back ACL injuries and is now a founding partner of *Going Steady*, a quarterback coach in the Phoenix valley, and a public speaker on mindset and resilience.

Find him on Instagram, TikTok, and LinkedIn — **@cstrevy5**.

title: What I Did Isn't Who I Am: Mark Immelman on Identity, the Masters, and the Four Pillars of Elite Performance
summary: Mark Immelman — PGA Tour coach, CBS Golf analyst, and brother of Masters champion Trevor Immelman — joins Seth for a wide-ranging conversation on what he sees in the best golfers in the world. The mental approach, the practice habits, the recovery rituals, and what separates the players who win from the players who finish second.
showNotes:
Mark Immelman has seen golf from every chair — two-time NCAA champion as a player, twenty-one seasons as a Division I head coach at Columbus State, swing coach to his brother Trevor through Trevor's 2008 Masters win, and now lead analyst at the CBS Golf tower. This conversation is what he's learned looking at greatness from each of those angles.

## Key takeaways

- **The governor on most golfers is fear of failure.** Mark won as a junior because he outlasted people. He didn't actually unlock until he stopped playing defensively in contention.
- **Gary Player's old line, reframed.** *"Hit every shot like it's the last one you'll ever hit."* As a kid that meant pressure. As an adult it meant: I'd focus harder. I'd prepare better. I wouldn't back down. Same words, opposite effect.
- **The greats welcome the mundane.** Tiger's zen state; Rory's exuberance; Scottie's flatline equanimity — different superpowers, same nauseating willingness to do boring fundamentals for hours.
- **Trevor's Masters week began with a ball marker on a green.** No three putters. No technical coaches. One controllable per putt: did you hit it solidly, did it cross your start line? "Yes" or "no." Three days of yeses going into the green jacket.
- **Performance lives at the intersection of four pillars.** Physical, mental, emotional, spiritual. Mark plays the whole chord, not just one note.

## Notable quotes

> "Winning the Masters is what I did. Winning the Masters is not who I am." — Larry Mize, after Mark asked why he writes a Bible verse on every autograph

> "God doesn't call the qualified; God qualifies the called."

> "It's not stupid aggressive. It's just not defensive all the time."

## Resources & references

- **Trevor Immelman's 2008 Masters win** — Mark's behind-the-scenes account of the putter-on-the-practice-green intervention
- **Gary Player** — the lessons Mark and Trevor absorbed growing up in South Africa; the "build a green in your backyard" advice that became Trevor's short game
- **Bob Jones (Bobby Jones)** — Mark's golf hero; the loop that closed when Trevor won at Augusta
- **Larry Mize** — 1987 Masters champion; Mark's teacher in the "who I am vs. what I do" distinction
- ***The On the Mark Golf Podcast*** — Mark's golf show; Seth was a previous guest
- ***The Greater Call*** — Mark's faith-and-leadership podcast: stories of biblical figures who were called before they were qualified
- **WM Phoenix Open** — context for the in-the-booth observations on tour pros

## About the guest

Mark Immelman is the lead golf analyst for CBS Golf, a long-time PGA teaching professional, two-time NCAA champion as a player at Columbus State, and former head coach at Columbus State for 21 seasons. He's the older brother of 2008 Masters champion Trevor Immelman and co-host of *The On the Mark Golf Podcast* and *The Greater Call*.

Find him on socials at **@mark_immelman** and at **markimmelman.com**.

title: "You'll Never Reach Your Potential Until…" — Ken Crenshaw on Greatness, Not-Enoughness, and 38 Years Inside Pro Baseball
summary: Ken Crenshaw spent 22 years as the head of Sports Medicine and Performance for the Arizona Diamondbacks. He talks with Seth about what he learned operating at the cutting edge of MLB sports medicine, working with hundreds of players, and the mental and physical disciplines that sustained him through two decades inside one of the most demanding performance operations in professional sport.
showNotes:
Ken Crenshaw didn't plan on baseball. He was a basketball player on a football scholarship at New Mexico State when he tore his ACL, found his way into the training room, and stumbled into a summer job with the Pittsburgh Pirates. Thirty-eight years later he's been with three franchises through five World Series runs, has worked with three of the last three-hundred-game winners in baseball history, and is twenty-two years into his role as Head of Sports Medicine and Performance for the Arizona Diamondbacks.

This conversation is what those thirty-eight years taught him about greatness, words, and team.

## Key takeaways

- **The greats use one of two engines.** "Not enoughness" — *I'll prove you wrong* — or pure competition with the work itself. Ken's seen both. Both can take you to the top. Only one is sustainable past it.
- **Resistance creates strength until it doesn't.** Stress is good until it's not. A loose, reactive body outperforms a tense one — and the loose body starts in the head.
- **Decouple the parent–kid loop.** Kids try to please their parents. Parents judge themselves by their kids' results. Both are trying to "win" the same mental reward and creating drag on each other. Ken's signature move with the families he coaches is naming the loop out loud.
- **Goldschmidt's depressurizer.** *"There are a lot of other places in the game I can help the team."* When his swing wasn't going well, Paul Goldschmidt found ways to contribute — running, defense, baserunning. Ken's biggest lesson about how the best handle pressure.
- **It's simple hard.** The simple things — pass, catch, box out, hit the start line on the putt — are easy to know and brutally difficult to do consistently. The greats stop looking for new hacks.

## Notable quotes

> "Words actually create the world that we're going to live in. Not the outside world — the inside world that we develop in our own minds and, subsequently, the emotions we feel."

> "There's more power in the team than there is in you. Once I learned that, it changed everything."

> "We don't see the world the way it is. We see the world the way we are."

## Resources & references

- **Coach Ron Becker** — the high school basketball coach who saw Ken's talent at fourteen, recommended he be cut from varsity, and changed his life with the conversation that followed
- **Paul Goldschmidt** — the dugout visualization habit; the swing/start-line drill; the team-first mentality under pressure
- **Earl Nightingale** — *"Attitude is the secret sauce"*
- **Greg Maddux, John Smoltz, Tom Glavine, Randy Johnson, Hank Aaron, Willie Stargell, Wade Boggs, Fred McGriff, Deion Sanders** — the supporting cast across Pittsburgh, Atlanta, Tampa Bay, and Arizona
- **The Ultimate Team Building Experience** — Ken's invite-only workshop for ~20 leaders at a time, covering selection, development, feedback, leadership, communication, and the trust that ties them together

## About the guest

Ken Crenshaw is the head of Sports Medicine and Performance for the Arizona Diamondbacks, where he has worked for 22 years. Before that he was strength and conditioning coordinator for the Atlanta Braves (including the 1995 World Series team) and the expansion Tampa Bay Rays. He grew up in Carrizozo, New Mexico (population: under 1,000), and is one of the most quietly influential figures in MLB sports medicine.

Reach him directly at **kcrenshaw@dbacks.com** to inquire about his workshops.

title: Feed the Right Dog | Jon Gordon on Rewiring Your Mind, Forgiveness & Living in the Zone
summary: The inaugural episode of Unlimit Your Potential. Bestselling author and leadership speaker Jon Gordon joins Seth Pepper for a conversation on positive leadership, the daily disciplines that compound, and what 25 years of coaching NFL teams, Fortune 500 companies, and championship organizations has taught him about what actually works.
showNotes:
Jon Gordon — author of *The Energy Bus* and 32 other books, advisor to the Dodgers, Heat, and Jaguars, the man literally known as "Mr. Positive" — is, by his own admission, naturally negative. He grew up in a Jewish-Italian household on Long Island with a NYC narcotics-detective father. He played lacrosse at Cornell terrified of mistakes. He spent his thirties on the edge of bankruptcy with a wife threatening to leave him because of how miserable he was to be around.

The work he's done his entire career has been figuring out how he became the person he is anyway. This conversation is the unvarnished version of it.

## Key takeaways

- **You feed the positive dog.** Every day, fear/worry/anxiety/doubt vs. courage/confidence/belief/optimism are fighting inside you. The one that wins is the one you feed.
- **You're not the thoughts you think — you're the thoughts you *believe*.** The first negative thought lands; you have the power of the second thought. Speak truth to the lie.
- **Talk to yourself, don't listen to yourself.** Dr. James Gills (six-time double Ironman finisher at age 59): if I listen to myself, I hear every reason I can't. If I talk to myself, I feed myself the words I need.
- **Forgive fast.** Holding bitterness keeps you tied to whoever wronged you. Release them; that's when you start growing. The woman who lost "195 pounds — that's how much my ex-boyfriend weighed when I forgave him."
- **The zone is the normal state.** Not a peak experience. Anxiety and disconnection are the abnormal states. The work is closing the gap.

## Notable quotes

> "Negative thoughts come from outside of you. You are not the thoughts you think; you are the thoughts you believe."

> "Forgiveness is the ultimate weight loss."

> "Adversity is our partner in growth. Energy vampires are our greatest test."

## The PRAYER framework

Jon's daily practice — works for any tradition or none:

- **P**raise — name what's amazing about the world you're in
- **R**epent — let go of the heavy energy you're carrying ("soul pain")
- **A**sk — ask for what you want, then let go of the outcome
- **Y**ield — surrender control; stop trying to be God
- **E**xpect — anticipate great things from a place of trust, not entitlement
- **R**eceive — open your hands and let it come

## Resources & references

- **Jon's books** — *The Energy Bus*, *The Carpenter*, *Training Camp*, *The Positive Dog*, *The Power of Positive Leadership*, *The One Truth*
- **Dr. James Gills** — six-time double Ironman finisher; the source of "talk to yourself instead of listening to yourself"
- **The "thank you" walk** — Jon's daily gratitude-and-prayer walk; he says nearly every book idea has come from one
- **The Jacksonville Jaguars 2024 camp** — "We've got some work to do" — Jon's account of asking how connected the team was on a 1-10 scale
- **Liam Coen** — current Jaguars head coach; Jon brought their conversation back into the loop on his own podcast
- **Tim Tebow** — Jon's example of the competitive spirit channeled into mission (Tim's anti-trafficking work)

## About the guest

Jon Gordon is the author of 33 books including the perennial bestseller *The Energy Bus*. He's worked with the Los Angeles Dodgers, the Miami Heat, the Jacksonville Jaguars, Pittsburgh Pirates, MLB's Tampa Bay Rays, Fortune 500 leadership teams, and countless coaches and athletes. He speaks roughly 100 times a year.

Connect with him at **jongordon.com** or on Instagram at **@JonGordon11**.
